scenes.text: You can work harder on your content and still lose people if the idea is fuzzy. The first scene has one job: make the viewer instantly recognize a problem they already feel.
The second scene explains the shift. Not ten lessons, not a full framework, just the one change that makes the problem feel solvable and the promise feel believable.
The final scene gives one next step. A good CTA should feel like relief, not pressure. Tell them exactly what to do and why doing it now helps.
If every new ad changes everything, your results cannot teach you much. A clean test keeps the offer steady and changes one part of the creative recipe.
Try three hooks for the same angle, or three pain points for the same hook. Now you are learning what moved the result instead of guessing from a pile of mixed changes.
Use a small board to track hook, angle, pain point, promise, and CTA. That is enough structure to improve without turning creative testing into a spreadsheet headache.
